Search results for: python asyncio gather exception handling

1. asyncio — Asynchronous I/O — Python 3.13 documentation
   https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html
   If return_exceptions is False (default), the first raised exception is immediately propagated to the task that awaits on gather(). If return_exceptions is True, exceptions are treated the same as successful results and aggregated in the result list.

2. python asyncio.gather(): Handle exceptions without cancelling other tasks
   https://stackoverflow.com/questions/58223401/python-asyncio-gather-handle-exceptions
   Use return_exceptions=True on gather(), then iterate results and isinstance(r, Exception) to branch. For cancellation behavior use asyncio.wait with FIRST_EXCEPTION instead.

3. A Deep Dive Into asyncio.gather() - Python Discord Blog
   https://pythondiscord.com/pages/guides/python-guides/asyncio-gather-deep-dive/
   Covers cancellation semantics, aiohttp integration, and why asyncio.TaskGroup (3.11+) is now preferred over gather() for structured concurrency.

4. PEP 654 – Exception Groups and except*
   https://peps.python.org/pep-0654/
   Enables handling of multiple exceptions raised concurrently, which pairs with TaskGroup.
Search results for: ruff check ignore specific rule line

1. Suppressing errors - Ruff
   https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/linter/#error-suppression
   Use `# noqa: E501` at end of line to ignore a specific rule. For file-wide, put `# ruff: noqa: E501` at the top of the file. Multiple rules: `# noqa: E501, F401`.

2. ruff noqa doesn't work - GitHub Issue #1991
   https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff/issues/1991
   Common gotcha: blank noqa (no rule code) silences everything. Prefer the scoped form `# noqa: E501` so future regressions still surface.

3. Configuring Ruff - Astral Docs
   https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/configuration/
   Global ignores via `[tool.ruff.lint] ignore = ["E501"]` in pyproject.toml. Per-file via `[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]`.
Search results for: tensorrt-llm blackwell sm_120 fp8 install

1. NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM Release Notes (v0.16)
   https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM/releases
   Native sm_120 (Blackwell consumer) FP8 kernels added in v0.15; v0.16 adds speculative decoding and improves GQA throughput. Requires CUDA 12.8+.

2. Building TensorRT-LLM from source on RTX 50-series
   https://nvidia.github.io/TensorRT-LLM/installation/build-from-source.html
   Set `TRTLLM_CUDA_ARCHS=120-real` before make. Prebuilt wheels from PyPI target sm_90/sm_100 only — sm_120 needs source build.

3. RTX 5070 Ti TensorRT-LLM fp8 benchmark thread - r/LocalLLaMA
   https://www.reddit.com/r/LocalLLaMA/comments/xxxxxx
   Users report 2.3–2.8x throughput over vLLM fp8 on Llama-3.3-70B at 16K context. Unsloth weights work after conversion via `convert_checkpoint.py --dtype fp8`.
Search results for: sqlalchemy select from multiple tables join

1. ORM Querying Guide — SQLAlchemy 2.0 Documentation
   https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/queryguide/select.html
   `select(User, Address).join(Address, User.id == Address.user_id)` — explicit join condition. For implicit FK-driven joins: `select(User).join(Address)`.

2. SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration: session.query is legacy
   https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/changelog/migration_20.html
   Replace `session.query(User).join(Address)` with `session.execute(select(User).join(Address)).scalars()`.

3. Efficient SQLAlchemy joins with selectinload
   https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/queryguide/relationships.html#selectin-eager-loading
   For N+1 avoidance on one-to-many, `selectinload(User.addresses)` issues one extra IN query rather than a wide outer join.
Search results for: pytest parametrize fixture dependency

1. Parametrizing fixtures and test functions — pytest docs
   https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/parametrize.html
   Use `@pytest.fixture(params=[...])` on the fixture — tests that receive it will run once per param. To share across tests, use `scope="module"` on the fixture.

2. Indirect parametrization — pytest docs
   https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/example/parametrize.html#indirect-parametrization
   `@pytest.mark.parametrize("my_fixture", [1, 2], indirect=True)` passes the param into the fixture body as `request.param`.

3. pytest-lazy-fixture (still third-party for now)
   https://github.com/TvoroG/pytest-lazy-fixture
   Needed if you want to pass fixture names themselves as parametrize values — pytest core does not support this natively.
Search results for: dockerfile multi-stage python slim non-root user

1. Best practices for writing Dockerfiles — Docker Docs
   https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/
   Use `FROM python:3.13-slim AS builder` to build wheels, then `FROM python:3.13-slim` final stage that `COPY --from=builder`. Add `USER 1000:1000` before CMD.

2. Python Docker images: security-first template - Snyk Blog
   https://snyk.io/blog/best-practices-containerizing-python-docker/
   Pin base by digest (`python@sha256:...`). `--no-install-recommends` on apt. `RUN useradd -r -u 1000 app`. Drop capabilities via compose.

3. Distroless Python images - Google
   https://github.com/GoogleContainerTools/distroless
   `gcr.io/distroless/python3-debian12` — no shell, minimal attack surface. Downside: harder to debug in prod without `debug` tag.
Search results for: fastapi middleware request id correlation

1. Custom Middleware - FastAPI docs
   https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/middleware/
   Example: generate UUID4, stash in request.state, add X-Request-ID header to response. Works with async request/response cycle.

2. asgi-correlation-id PyPI package
   https://pypi.org/project/asgi-correlation-id/
   Drop-in: `app.add_middleware(CorrelationIdMiddleware)`. Also injects into logging.LogRecord via ContextVar so `%correlation_id` works in log format.

3. OpenTelemetry FastAPI instrumentation
   https://opentelemetry-python-contrib.readthedocs.io/
   `FastAPIInstrumentor.instrument_app(app)` auto-adds traceparent header — use that instead of a bespoke request id when running with OTEL collector.
Search results for: langchain vs langgraph difference

1. LangGraph: Stateful, orchestrated workflows - LangChain Blog
   https://blog.langchain.dev/langgraph/
   LangChain is a toolkit of primitives (prompts, retrievers, runnables). LangGraph is a cyclic-DAG orchestrator on top of those primitives — built for multi-turn, stateful agents with persistence.

2. When to reach for LangGraph vs LangChain - r/LangChain
   https://www.reddit.com/r/LangChain/comments/xxxxxx
   LangChain: one-shot RAG or linear chains. LangGraph: agent loops, human-in-the-loop, conditional routing, durable state.

3. LangGraph Core Concepts docs
   https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/
   Nodes + edges + shared state object. Checkpointers persist across steps; interrupts enable human approval.
Search results for: pydantic v2 model_config strict mode

1. Model Config - Pydantic docs
   https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/#model-configuration
   `model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True)` disables type coercion — `"42"` will NOT be coerced to `42` for an `int` field. Raises ValidationError instead.

2. Strict mode per-field override
   https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/strict_mode/
   `Field(strict=False)` on individual fields overrides a strict model config. Useful when 90% of your fields should be strict.

3. Pydantic v1 → v2 migration: class Config is deprecated
   https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/migration/
   Replace inner `class Config` with module-level `model_config = ConfigDict(...)`. The v1 form still works but emits DeprecationWarning.
Search results for: postgresql 18 jsonb gin index performance

1. JSON Types - PostgreSQL 18 docs
   https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/datatype-json.html
   `CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl USING GIN (col jsonb_path_ops)` gives fastest lookups for `@>` containment queries. Indexes about 40% smaller than the default `jsonb_ops` opclass.

2. PG18 improvements to JSONB - Release notes
   https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18.html
   Adds `jsonb_transform()` and SIMD-accelerated `jsonb_path_query` — up to 3x on large documents.

3. When JSONB GIN indexes don't help - Supabase Blog
   https://supabase.com/blog/jsonb-gin-not-always-fastest
   Queries that use `->>` key extraction + equality fall back to full scans. Use an expression index on the extracted path instead.
Search results for: react server component vs client component 2026

1. React Server Components — react.dev
   https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components
   Default in App Router since Next.js 14. No hooks, no event handlers, no client JS shipped. Mark interactive islands with `"use client"`.

2. When to use RSC vs client components - Next.js docs
   https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/rendering/composition-patterns
   Data fetching → server. Interactivity/state/effects → client. Prefer passing server-rendered children into client boundary as props to keep client bundle small.

3. Server Actions - Vercel blog
   https://vercel.com/blog/react-server-actions
   `"use server"` flagged async function callable from client — eliminates most bespoke API route code for mutations.

   Most recent confirmed release is Qwen3.5 Max (Jan 2026). No Qwen 4.5 roadmap published as of April 2026.

2. Qwen release history - HuggingFace model page

Search results for: git rebase interactive squash multiple commits

1. git rebase - interactive mode - git-scm.com
   https://git-scm.com/docs/git-rebase#_interactive_mode
   `git rebase -i HEAD~5` opens editor with the 5 most recent commits. Change `pick` to `squash` (or `s`) to fold a commit into the one above it. `fixup` discards the squashed commit's message.

2. GitHub: "Squash and merge" vs local squash
   https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/incorporating-changes-from-a-pull-request/about-pull-request-merges#squash-and-merge-your-commits
   If your team uses squash-merge on PRs, local squashing is usually unnecessary — the merge button does it for you.

3. git autosquash - Atlassian tutorials
   https://www.atlassian.com/git/tutorials/rewriting-history
   `git commit --fixup=<sha>` + `git rebase -i --autosquash` automates the squash-into-earlier-commit workflow.
Search results for: torch.compile graph break debugging

1. torch._dynamo.explain() — PyTorch docs
   https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html
   Wrap your function with `torch._dynamo.explain(fn)(*args)` to get a full breakdown of graph breaks with the source lines that caused them.

2. TORCH_LOGS=graph_breaks env var
   https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/logging.html
   `TORCH_LOGS="+dynamo,graph_breaks"` prints every break reason at runtime. Common cause: numpy/ctypes calls inside the compiled region.

3. torch.compile FAQ - PyTorch forum
   https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/torch-compile-mechanism/199
   Dict mutation, list.pop, and `.item()` are frequent break causes. `mode="reduce-overhead"` catches more than the default mode.
Search results for: prometheus histogram vs summary when to use

1. Histograms and summaries - Prometheus docs
   https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/histograms/
   Histograms aggregate across instances (good for PromQL quantiles). Summaries compute quantiles client-side and do NOT aggregate — never use for cluster-wide latency.

2. Native histograms (experimental, Prom 2.40+)
   https://prometheus.io/docs/concepts/metric_types/#histogram
   Eliminates bucket choice — automatic log-linear buckets. Still requires opt-in with `--enable-feature=native-histograms`.

3. Grafana SLO dashboards with histogram_quantile
   https://grafana.com/tutorials/slo-dashboard/
   `histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le) (rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])))` — canonical p99 latency query.
Search results for: openai function calling json mode strict

1. Structured outputs - OpenAI API docs
   https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/structured-outputs
   `response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": {..., "strict": true}}` guarantees the model never emits JSON that fails the schema. Requires gpt-4o-2024-08-06 or newer.

2. Tool choice = required
   https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/chat/create#chat-create-tool_choice
   `tool_choice="required"` forces the model to call at least one of the provided tools — useful as a hard guardrail for agents.

3. instructor library wraps this idiomatically
   https://python.useinstructor.com/
   `client.chat.completions.create(..., response_model=MyPydanticModel)` — retries on schema violation, returns typed object.
Search results for: grpo trl trainer loss_type dapo beta

1. GRPOTrainer - TRL docs (0.29)
   https://huggingface.co/docs/trl/v0.29.1/en/grpo_trainer
   `loss_type="dapo"` with `beta=0.0` removes the KL penalty. Recommended for small (<4B) models where KL dominates and causes collapse.

2. DAPO paper: Direct Advantage Policy Optimization
   https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.xxxxx
   Original DAPO formulation; TRL's implementation tracks the paper closely. Key is the token-level clipping range.

3. Unsloth + TRL GRPO example notebook
   https://github.com/unslothai/unsloth/blob/main/examples/GRPO_Notebook.ipynb
   Concrete config that has been load-tested on a 16GB GPU. Uses `bf16=True`, NOT fp8 — fp8 is inference-only in TRL 0.29.
Search results for: chromadb persistent client embedding function

1. ChromaDB PersistentClient - Chroma docs
   https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/collections/manage-collections
   `chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma")` — stores SQLite + parquet under the given directory. Survives process restarts.

2. embedding_function collection-level
   https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/embeddings/embedding-functions
   Set once on `create_collection(..., embedding_function=ef)`. Chroma calls it for you on `.add(documents=...)`. Mixing embedding models across collections is fine; within a collection is not.

3. SentenceTransformerEmbeddingFunction
   https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/embeddings/embedding-functions#sentence-transformers
   Wrapper around sentence-transformers. For BGE / e5 / jina models, instantiate the EmbeddingFunction once at startup — model load is the slow part.
Search results for: rust tokio select macro cancel_safe futures

1. tokio::select! macro - Tokio docs
   https://docs.rs/tokio/latest/tokio/macro.select.html
   All branches must be cancel-safe — futures that are dropped when another branch completes must not leave broken state. Async mutex lock acquisition is NOT cancel-safe.

2. What makes a future cancel-safe - tokio blog
   https://tokio.rs/blog/2020-04-preemption
   Rule of thumb: reading a single item (e.g. from a channel) is typically safe; writing mid-transaction (partial DB write) is not. Wrap unsafe branches in `tokio::pin!` + state machine.

3. Common gotcha: reading from Stream inside select
   https://users.rust-lang.org/t/tokio-select-stream/xxxxx
   Use `StreamExt::next()` and handle None explicitly — dropping a Stream mid-`.next()` loses the item.
Search results for: kubernetes pod stuck terminating force delete

1. Pods stuck in Terminating - Kubernetes docs
   https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/force-delete-stateful-set-pod/
   `kubectl delete pod <name> --grace-period=0 --force` — removes the etcd entry even if kubelet never reports back. May leak resources on the node.

2. Why pods stuck terminating - Red Hat Blog
   https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/pods-terminating-kubernetes
   Common causes: PodDisruptionBudget preventing eviction; finalizer not being removed; kubelet offline; volume unmount hanging.

3. Remove stuck finalizer
   https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/finalizers/
   `kubectl patch pod <name> -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":null}}'` — last-resort; make sure the controller that owns the finalizer is actually gone.
